title: OVMS continuous-batching concurrency sweep — Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B INT4 on Arc 140V (dispatch best-of-N parallelism, #695)
purpose: Measure whether best-of-N coder candidates can run CONCURRENTLY via OVMS continuous batching on the integrated Arc 140V (shared LPDDR5X, no discrete VRAM), and find the binding constraint (KV-cache vs compute).

hardware.gpu: Intel Arc 140V (Xe2), 16 GB shared from 32 GB LPDDR5X

methodology.concurrency: N identical requests fired simultaneously via a thread pool (max_workers=N)
methodology.warmup: one request before each sweep to remove compile/first-token cost
methodology.telemetry: OVMS server-log llm_executor ticks: 'All requests / Scheduled requests / cache usage %'
note_not_measured: Full multi-minute opencode AGENT-RUN concurrency (only raw single-request batching measured; an agent run is many sequential requests, so candidate-concurrency ~= one in-flight request per candidate, which this approximates).
Per-candidate SOLVE-RATE under concurrency (a quality, not throughput, question).
N > 8, and cache_size / precision tuning beyond the shipped config.

findings: Continuous batching ENGAGES on the integrated Arc 140V: OVMS scheduled up to 7-8 concurrent sequences; aggregate throughput rose 1.87x at N=4 and 2.37x at N=8 (short prompts).

The KV cache is NOT the binding constraint for best-of-N: 8 concurrent 6,675-token contexts used only 10.5% of the 4 GB pool — because enable_prefix_caching stores the SHARED best-of-N prompt once (and u8 halves KV bytes). The cache could hold far more.

The binding constraint is COMPUTE: the integrated GPU saturates (~40-90 tok/s aggregate); concurrency speedup is real but SUB-LINEAR, and per-request latency grows with N (5.1s -> 17.2s at N=8 short).

Net for best-of-N: running candidates concurrently reduces wall-clock to first/best result by ~1.9x (N=4) to ~2.4x (N=8), cache-cheap. The sweet spot trades the sub-linear speedup against the growing per-candidate latency.
recommendation: Parallelism is VIABLE and cache-cheap. Recommend wiring concurrency as a config knob defaulting to C=1 (sequential, today's behavior), with C=2-3 as the proposed production default once a live concurrent dispatch is signed off — the LA decides the default (a resource/behavior posture call). C beyond ~4 buys little (compute-bound) and grows per-candidate latency.
